attrs.alt: Callbacks -- synchronous (blocking stack) vs asynchronous (event loop); callback hell and modern alternatives

content.text: is a function passed as an argument to another function, to be called at a later point — either immediately (synchronous) or when an operation completes (asynchronous). Callbacks are the oldest pattern for dealing with asynchronous operations in JavaScript and remain fundamental even in modern code.
When a callback is called immediately within the same stack frame, it is
When a callback is scheduled to run
the current execution context finishes, it is
. The JavaScript engine uses a single-threaded
: asynchronous work (timers, network requests, I/O) is offloaded to the browser/Node.js, and the callback is queued when the work completes.
) convention: the first argument to every asynchronous callback is an error object (
on success), and the second is the result. This became a widely adopted pattern.
Always handle errors in async callbacks. If you ignore the first argument, errors are swallowed silently and the app continues in an invalid state.
When each async operation depends on the result of the previous one, callbacks nest deeply — this is called
. The code becomes hard to read, maintain, and test.
Extracting named functions reduces nesting but does not eliminate the callback-based control flow.

Named functions are good for readability, but the fundamental problem — no return values, no try/catch, manual error threading — remains. Promises and async/await solve these issues at the language level.
to wrap any error-first callback function into a Promise-based one. In the browser you can write a generic wrapper.

Errors swallowed; app continues in broken state
Always check if (err) as the first thing in a callback
Returning a value from an async callback expecting it to propagate
Return value is discarded; caller already returned
Use Promises or pass results via another callback/side-channel
Callback continues firing after component is destroyed (memory leak, state mutation)
Clean up in ngOnDestroy or use RxJS takeUntilDestroyed
Calling this inside an arrow callback without binding
Arrow functions capture this from declaration scope -- usually correct
Use arrow functions; avoid function() in callbacks unless you need a different this
Mixing callbacks and Promises in the same chain
Error handling becomes inconsistent; unhandled rejections or swallowed errors
Pick one pattern for a pipeline; use promisify to convert legacy callbacks
utilities to wrap any callback-based API into an Observable stream.
Angular's testing utilities make async callbacks predictable via
callback parameter for truly async tests.
Callbacks are the foundation of asynchronous programming in JavaScript. Key points:
A callback is a function passed to another function to be called later
Synchronous callbacks (forEach, map, filter) run immediately in the call stack
Asynchronous callbacks (setTimeout, event listeners, I/O) are queued in the event loop and run after the current stack clears
The error-first convention: first arg is error (null on success), second is result
Callback hell arises from deeply nested async operations; extract named functions or migrate to Promises
Promises and async/await solve callback hell at the language level; RxJS Observables are Angular's preferred pattern for streams
In Angular: clean up timers and intervals in ngOnDestroy; run high-frequency callbacks outside the Angular zone
